content.problemStatement:
You are given a **binary** string SSS of length NNN.

You can perform the following two-step operation on it:


Choose a non-empty contiguous substring of SSS.
If the substring contains an odd number of ones, delete every 000 in it.

If it instead contains an even number of ones, delete every 111 in it.
For example, suppose S=1010001S = \texttt{1010001}S=1010001,


If you choose the substring 101\texttt{101}101 from indices 111 to 333, it has two ones, which is an even number.

So, delete every 111 in the substring, turning the string into S=00001S = \texttt{00001}S=00001.
If you instead choose the substring 1000\texttt{1000}1000 from indices 333 to 666, the number of ones is 111, which is odd.

So, you'd delete every 000 in the substring, turning the string into S=1011S = \texttt{1011}S=1011.
You can perform this two-step operation as many times as you like.

Find the **minimum** possible length of the final string.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the length of the binary string.
The second line contains the binary string SSS of length NNN.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum possible length of SSS after performing several operations.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 3⋅1053\cdot 10^53⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Choose the substring 11\texttt{11}11, which is the entire string.
It has two ones, which is even - so we delete every occurrence of 111. The string becomes empty.
Test case 222: One optimal sequence of operations is as follows:

Choose the substring 10\texttt{10}10 from index 222 to index 333. It has one 111, which is an odd number - so every occurrence of 000 gets deleted. The string is now 01\texttt{01}01.
Choose the entire string, which is 01\texttt{01}01. The 000 gets deleted again, making the string 1\texttt{1}1.

This is optimal.
